questions.question:
Re-read the following line from paragraph 14:

“Except when math period ends Mrs. Price says loud and in front of everybody, ‘Now, Rachel, that's enough,’ because she sees I've shoved the red sweater to the tippy-tip corner of my desk and it's hanging over the edge like a waterfall, but I don't care.”

What specific characteristic do waterfalls and the hanging sweater share that makes this comparison effective?

responses.content: She compared it to a waterfall

questions.question: In paragraph 19, Rachel says she's “crying like I'm three in front of everybody.” This comparison is an example of:

responses.content: They compare because the water from  a waterfall hag from the edge like the sweater

questions.question: How does the figurative language in paragraph 14 help readers understand Rachel's reaction to the sweater?

responses.content: D. The simile in the paragraph makes it clear to readers that Rachel is overreacting about the sweater.

questions.question: How does the figurative language in paragraph 19 help readers visualize Rachel's behavior?

responses.content: C. The similes about her body shaking “like when you have the hiccups” and her head hurting “like when you drink milk too fast” connect to unpleasant experiences most readers have had.

questions.question:
Throughout the excerpt, Sal watches the Winterbottom family interact and then reflects on what matters, ‘in the course of a lifetime.’

According to the last paragraph, how does watching the Winterbottom family help Sal understand the difference between things that matter and things that don't matter in life? Use evidence from the text to support your response.

responses.content: SAL LEARNED THAT THE SMALL THINGS IN LIFE DON’T MATTER THE TEXT SAID SHE WAS CLEANING OUT HER DESK INSTEAD OF GOING ON A WALK WITH HER DIEING MOM.

SAL LEARNED THAT THE SMALL THINGS IN LIFE DON’T MATTER THE TEXT SAID SHE WAS CLEANING OUT HER DESK INSTEAD OF GOING ON A WALK WITH HER DIEING MOM AND Mrs. Winterbottom suddenly leaned over and kissed Phoebe's cheek.
 "I'm not a baby, you know," Phoebe said, wiping off the kiss.
Mrs. Winterbottom stabbed the brownies with a knife. "Want one?" she asked. "They're burned," Phoebe said. "Besides, I'm fat."
 "Oh sweetie, you're not fat," Mrs. Winterbottom said.
"I am."
 "No, you're not."
Then Phoebe's sister, Prudence, stormed into the house, slamming the door behind her. "I blew it. I just know it!" she wailed.
 "Oh sweetie," her mother said.
I did!" Prudence said. "I did, I did, I did."
Mrs. Winterbottom half-heartedly chipped away at the burned brownies and asked Prudence if she would have another chance at cheerleading tryouts.
 "Yes, tomorrow. But I know I'm going to blow it!
"Her mother said, "Maybe I'll come along and watch." I could tell that Mrs. Winterbottom was trying to rise above some awful sadness she was feeling, but Prudence couldn't see that. Prudence had her own agenda, just as I had had my own agenda that day my mother wanted me to walk with her. I couldn't see my own mother's sadness.
What?" Prudence said. "Come along and watch ?"
 "Yes, wouldn't that be nice?"
 "No!" Prudence said. "No, no, no. You can't. It would be awful."

activities.text_title: Heroes Every Child Should Know: Perseus

responses.content: perseus rapped her head   in goat  skin and flew away

responses.content: Perseus action show us that  he followed athenes plan because he got to Medusa did not look at her but used a mirror to slay her

questions.question: In paragraph 49, Perseus calls the sandals “brave.” What does this reveal about Perseus' character in this moment?

responses.content: he is turning to the sandals  and tells them to save him  which means
means that he is scared

questions.question: Based on Perseus's actions in paragraphs 46 through 47, the reader can conclude that—

responses.content: C. Hermes will be saddened over the resolution of the plot's conflict.

questions.question: When Perseus's blood runs cold in paragraph 49, it reveals that he has—

responses.content: C. brief doubt in Athene's plan.

questions.question: Which phrase from paragraph 29 best signals the author's use of flashback?

responses.content: Percy is concerned mom is sending him to another school
responses.bucket_rationale: The response directly identifies Percy's concern as his mom sending him to another school, which matches the key scene elements in paragraphs 24-28. The answer is specific, text-based, and does not contradict any details from the excerpt.

questions.question: How did the author structure the flashback in paragraphs 29-32?

responses.content: B. flood of memories came back to me

questions.question: How do paragraphs 30-31 support Percy's statement?

responses.content: the author structure the flash backs backwards
responses.bucket_rationale: The response 'the author structure the flash backs backwards' directly identifies a key structural element of the flashback: that the memories are presented in reverse chronological order (backwards). This matches the text, which starts with a memory from third grade and then goes further back to preschool. The answer is concise but clearly addresses the structure, not just the content, and does not contradict the text. Therefore, it meets the threshold for 'correct.'

questions.question: Why might the author choose to use the flashbacks in paragraphs 30 and 31?

responses.content: D. to reinforce the idea that Percy is different and unique

questions.question: Which sentence or phrase from the passage gives the clearest understanding that the story will be about Percy's struggles?

responses.content: B. “In every single school, something creepy had happened, something unsafe, and I was forced to move.”

questions.question: Reread the dialogue in paragraphs 14-16. How does learning this truth about his father affect Percy's feelings toward his father?

responses.content: Percy sees himself as different because he is dyslexic, hyperactive and gets bad grades in school
responses.bucket_rationale: The response clearly identifies that Percy sees himself as different because of his dyslexia, hyperactivity, and poor grades. This matches the negative self-perception described in paragraph 12, fulfilling both the form and accuracy requirements for the 'correct' bucket.

questions.question: Which moment in this excerpt represents the most significant turning point for Percy?

responses.content: He was sad because he thot that his dad only spent one summer with him
responses.bucket_rationale: The response shows text-specific engagement and recognizes that Percy is sad about his father only spending one summer with him. However, it does not mention the more specific emotions of anger, resentment, or disappointment that are depicted in the text. The answer is too general and misses the key emotional nuance, so it fits the 'partial-understanding' bucket.

responses.content: He felted angry
responses.bucket_rationale: The response 'He felted angry' directly identifies the emotion Percy feels toward his father after learning the truth, which matches the emotion depicted in the text (anger and resentment). The answer uses a specific emotion word, aligns with the text, and does not contradict any key scene elements. The language is age-appropriate and clearly demonstrates understanding.

questions.question: Reread paragraphs 20-26 where Percy and his mother discuss being sent away. How does the author create tension in this scene?

responses.content: B. When Percy learns his father left before he was born in paragraph 16

questions.question: Based on paragraph 3, what can you infer about how Margaret felt about her work as a photojournalist?

responses.content: you can
responses.bucket_rationale: The response 'you can' matches the exact motto given in the text. It is accurate and directly answers the question about the motto Margaret's parents gave her. There is no evidence of misunderstanding or fabrication.

questions.question: Highlight ONE sentence or phrase that shows an example of when Margaret Bourke-White's hardworking attitude helped advance her career.

responses.content: happy
responses.bucket_rationale: The response 'happy' expresses a positive feeling toward Margaret's work as a photojournalist, which aligns with the text's depiction of her as passionate, dedicated, and a trailblazer. The answer is brief but meets the threshold for expressing a positive orientation toward her work, as required for the 'correct' bucket.

questions.question: Based on paragraphs 10-12, what can you infer was important to Margaret as a photojournalist?

responses.content: Margaret solved this problem by bringing along a new style of flare (which produces white light) and having assistants hold them to light her scenes. Her abilities resulted in some of the best steel factory pictures of that era, and these earned her national attention.
responses.bucket_rationale: The student's highlight is: "Margaret solved this problem by bringing along a new style of flare (which produces white light) and having assistants hold them to light her scenes. Her abilities resulted in some of the best steel factory pictures of that era, and these earned her national attention." This is within the acceptable answer set, directly demonstrates Margaret's hardworking attitude and how it helped her career, and is within the character limit. The highlight is a direct answer to the question.

questions.question: Based on paragraph 14, how did Margaret influence future generations of women?

responses.content: maragaret wanted to bring complex events to people and make them muderstandable
responses.bucket_rationale: The response accurately identifies that Margaret wanted to bring complex events to people and make them understandable, which aligns with the text's description of her work as a photojournalist in paragraphs 10-12. The answer is specific, text-based, and matches the key scene elements, meeting both form and accuracy requirements for 'correct.'

questions.question: Which of the following selections best states the central idea of this biography of Margaret Bourke-White?

responses.content: D. Margaret is a great example for women because she built a successful career in a mostly male profession.

questions.question: Which statement from the text most strongly supports the answer to the previous question?

responses.content: A. “She proved to women that they had every right to pursue the careers they wanted.”
